title: Sampling Aware Ancestral State Inference
text: vignette demonstrates use saasi package ancestral state reconstruction. SAASI (Sampling-Aware Ancestral State Inference) ancestral state reconstruction method accounts variation sampling rates among locations traits. Unlike traditional methods assume uniform sampling, saasi explicitly models heterogeneous sampling, leading ancestral state estimates take sampling account. SAASI described Song et al. https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.05.20.655151v2.

title: Sampling Aware Ancestral State Inference
text: can install development version saasi GitHub :
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("MAGPIE-SFU/saasi")

title: Sampling Aware Ancestral State Inference
text: saasi function requires three main inputs: phylogenetic tree (class phylo) : Rooted binary branch lengths units time (positive) Contains tip states (tree$tip.state) missing values SAASI function prepare_tree_for_saasi() can ensure conditions met. Transition rate matrix Q (class matrix): Rates transition states Use estimate_transition_rates() estimate matrix. Birth-death-sampling parameters (class data.frame): Speciation rate Extinction/removal rate Sampling rate Use estimate_bds_parameters() estimate speciation rate single, overall sampling rate. “extinction rate” (language birth-death models) infectious disease applications inverse duration infectiousness, .e. rate leaving infectious class. output data frame containing probability state internal node phylogenetic tree.

previous_headings: Example: Ebola 2013-2016 West African Ebola Epidemic
title: Sampling Aware Ancestral State Inference
text: example, use data Nextstrain: https://nextstrain.org/ebola/ebov-2013?c=country. First, create data frame containing strains states. first column data frame match tree$tip.label, second column contain state interest. Users can skip step tree$tip.state already exists.
code: tree <- ape::read.tree(system.file("extdata", "nexstrain_ebola_ebov-2013_smaller.nwk", package = "saasi")) metadata <- readr::read_tsv(system.file("extdata", "nextstrain_ebola_ebov-2013_metadata.tsv", package = "saasi")) #> Rows: 1493 Columns: 8 #> ── Column specification ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Delimiter: "\t" #> chr  (7): strain, country, division, author, author__url, accession, accessi... #> date (1): date #>  #> ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data. #> ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message. tip_data <- data.frame(   tip_label = metadata$strain,   state = metadata$country )

previous_headings: Example: Ebola 2013-2016 West African Ebola Epidemic
what: Modify the tree to be compatible with saasi
title: Sampling Aware Ancestral State Inference
text: Users can apply function prepare_tree_for_saasi() make tree compatible saasi. check tree compatible, use function check_tree_compatibility(). , function include tip states (.e. location) tree tree downsampled version 2013 Ebola virus tree nextstrain. 193 tips three countries.
code: ebola_tree <- prepare_tree_for_saasi(tree, tip_data) plot_saasi(ebola_tree, saasi_result = NULL,tip_cex = 1)

previous_headings: Example: Ebola 2013-2016 West African Ebola Epidemic
title: Sampling Aware Ancestral State Inference
text: Users can estimate transition rate matrix Q using function estimate_transition_rates(). Users can specify model transition rate matrix: 1. Equal rate ER, 2. Symmetric rate SYM, 3. rates different ARD, Custom custom_q.
code: Q <- estimate_transition_rates(ebola_tree, method = 'fitMk', matrix_structure = 'SYM')

previous_headings: Example: Ebola 2013-2016 West African Ebola Epidemic
title: Sampling Aware Ancestral State Inference
text: Users can estimate speciation sampling rates using function estimate_bds_parameters(). infectious disease applications, users knowledge expected removal rate (1/μ1/\mu) disease interest, saasi requires extinction rate input (overcome identifiability limitations). obtain estimates overall speciation sampling rates, users knowledge maximum minimum R0, well upper lower bounds total removal rate (1/(μ+ψ))(1/(\mu+\psi)). Ebola, assume total infectious period (1/(μ+ψ))(1/(\mu+\psi)) 20 40 days. Converting years, total removal rate (μ+ψ\mu + \psi) 9.125 (365/40) 18.25 (365/20). assume μ=5\mu = 5, gives us bounds overall sampling rate ψ\psi 4.125 13.25. example, assume R0 1.5 3. Now set state-specific sampling pass parameters SAASI. overall sampling rate estimated (comparable λ\lambda μ\mu); SAASI allows us account state-specific relative sampling. Suppose, example, Liberia known sequenced smaller fraction cases states, factor 2.
code: rates <- estimate_bds_parameters(     ebola_tree,     mu = 5,      r0_max = 3,      r0_min = 1.5,     psi_max = 15,     infectious_period_min = 20/365, # convert days to years     infectious_period_max = 40/365, # convert days to years     n_starts = 100)

previous_headings: Example: Ebola 2013-2016 West African Ebola Epidemic
title: Sampling Aware Ancestral State Inference
code: pars = data.frame(state = colnames(Q), lambda=rates$lambda, mu = rates$mu, psi= rates$psi, row.names = NULL) pars[(pars$state=="Liberia"),"psi"] = pars[(pars$state=="Liberia"), "psi"]/2 # set sampling rate to be 1/2 the baseline

previous_headings: Example: Ebola 2013-2016 West African Ebola Epidemic
title: Sampling Aware Ancestral State Inference
code: saasi_ebola <- saasi(ebola_tree, Q, pars) #> Tree is compatible with SAASI

code: p1 <- plot_saasi(ebola_tree, saasi_ebola, tip_cex = 1, node_cex=0.3)

previous_headings: Example: Ebola 2013-2016 West African Ebola Epidemic
title: Sampling Aware Ancestral State Inference
text: saasi() function returns data frame probability distributions states internal node. Higher probabilities indicate greater confidence ancestral state. example, assume Liberia sampled lower rate two countries, see internal nodes inferred Liberia sampling locations. SAASI accounts undetected cases Liberia. Note Liberia factor 2 chosen entirely random purposes vignette represent knowledge opinion authors relative sampling outbreak.

title: SAASI Simulation Study
text: vignette demonstrates accuracy SAASI (Sampling-Aware Ancestral State Inference) simulation studies. simulate phylogenetic trees known ancestral states using birth-death-sampling process, assess well SAASI can recover internal states.

title: SAASI Simulation Study
text: Load required packages:
code: library(saasi)

title: SAASI Simulation Study
text: use 3-state model heterogeneous sampling rates test SAASI’s ability handle sampling bias: Key feature: State 1 sampling rate 0.1, states 2 3 sampling rates 1.0. creates sampling bias SAASI designed handle.
code: # Define birth-death-sampling parameters demo_pars <- data.frame(   state = c("1", "2", "3"),    rootprior = c(1/3, 1/3, 1/3),    lambda = c(3, 1.5, 1.5),      # Birth rate, also varies across states   mu = c(0.1, 0.1, 0.1),          # Death rate   psi = c(0.1, 1.0, 1.0)          # Sampling rate (heterogeneous!) )  # Define transition rate matrix Q demo_Q <- matrix(0.3, 3, 3) diag(demo_Q) <- -0.6 rownames(demo_Q) <- c("1", "2", "3") colnames(demo_Q) <- c("1", "2", "3")

title: SAASI Simulation Study
text: First, let’s demonstrate single simulation understand workflow: Extract true ancestral states (ground truth): Run SAASI infer ancestral states: Calculate accuracy:
code: set.seed(123)  # Generate tree with built-in post-processing phy <- sim_bds_tree(   params_df = demo_pars,    q_matrix = demo_Q,    x0 = 1,                    # Start at state 1   max_taxa = 500,             # Initial tree size   max_t = 50,                # Maximum time depth   min_tip = 100               # Minimum tips after post-processing ) # The true internal node states are stored in node.state true_states <- phy$node.state # Run SAASI  saasi_result <- saasi(phy = phy,              # phylogenetic tree                      Q = demo_Q,       # transition rate matrix                      pars = demo_pars) predicted_states <- apply(saasi_result, 1, which.max)  # Calculate accuracy accuracy <- mean(predicted_states == true_states) cat("Accuracy for this simulation:", round(accuracy, 3), "\n") #> Accuracy for this simulation: 0.9

title: SAASI Simulation Study
text: contrast, standard tools designed account differences sampling. Inferring ancestral states ace: Let’s plot truth, ace predictions saasi predictions:  SAASI much closer truth ace, accounts lower sampling state 1 (red). consequence lower sampling (course) red tips less well-represented tree process created tree. Even observed tips states 2 3, internal nodes giving rise might well state 1. SAASI correctly estimates many red internal nodes top bottom portions tree, whereas ace’s simpler discrete trait phylogeography model . SAASI’s likelihood computation accounts fact clade red ancestor still likely quite green blue tips , since sampled higher rate red tips.
code: ace_result = ace(phy$tip.state, phy, type="d") ace_predictions = apply(ace_result$lik.anc, 1, which.max) true_result = 0*saasi_result for (k in 1:nrow(true_result)) { true_result[k, true_states[k]] = 1 } op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(1, 1, 3, 1), oma = c(0, 0, 0, 0)) on.exit(par(op), add = TRUE) plot_saasi(phy, true_result, tip_cex = 1, node_cex = 0.6); title("Truth")  plot_saasi(phy, saasi_result, tip_cex = 1, node_cex = 0.6); title("SAASI") plot_saasi(phy, ace_result$lik.anc, tip_cex = 1, node_cex = 0.6); title("ACE")

what: Estimate rate parameters of a birth-death-sampling process — estimate_bds_parameters
title: Estimate rate parameters of a birth-death-sampling process — estimate_bds_parameters
text: estimate_bds_parameters() estimates speciation rate \(\lambda\) sampling rate \(\psi\) birth-death-sampling process given phylogenetic tree death rate \(\mu\). function uses user-specified epidemiological constraints sampling rate, basic reproduction number, duration infectious period improve estimability.

title: Estimate rate parameters of a birth-death-sampling process — estimate_bds_parameters
code: estimate_bds_parameters(   phy,   mu,   trim = c(0.1, 0.5),   n_starts = 100,   force_two_step = FALSE,   psi_max = 7,   r0_min = 1,   r0_max = 5,   infectious_period_min = NULL,   infectious_period_max = NULL )

title: Estimate rate parameters of a birth-death-sampling process — estimate_bds_parameters
text: phy object class phylo branch lengths. Must rooted binary. mu extinction rate. must non-negative. trim numeric vector length 2 specifying quantiles removing node times lineages--time regression (see Details). Default value c(0.10, 0.50). n_starts number starting points multi-start optimization. Default value 100. force_two_step Logical. TRUE, uses two-step estimates even violate constraints (see Details). Default value FALSE. psi_max Maximum sampling rate per unit time. Must strictly positive. Default value 7. r0_min Minimum basic reproductive number (R0 = lambda/(mu+psi)). Must strictly positive. Default 1. r0_max Maximum basic reproductive number. Must strictly positive. Default 5. infectious_period_min Minimum infectious period (1/(mu+psi)). Must strictly positive. Default NULL. infectious_period_max Maximum infectious period (1/(mu+psi)). Must strictly positive. Default NULL.

title: Estimate rate parameters of a birth-death-sampling process — estimate_bds_parameters
text: list following attributes: lambda: Estimated speciation rate psi: Estimated sampling rate mu: Input death rate r0: Estimated basic reproductive number (\(\lambda/(\mu+\psi)\)) infectious_period: Estimated infectious period (\(1/(\mu+\psi)\)) net_diversification: Estimated net diversification rate (\(\lambda-\mu-\psi\))

title: Estimate rate parameters of a birth-death-sampling process — estimate_bds_parameters
text: Estimation carried first estimating reproduction number \(\frac{\lambda}{\mu+\psi}\) net diversification rate \(\lambda-\mu-\psi\), solving unknown \(\lambda\) \(\psi\). net diversification rate estimated performing lineages--time (LTT) regression node times within quantiles specified trim input reproduction number estimated first computing maximum likelihood estimates (MLEs) \(\lambda\) \(\psi\) numerically optimizing likelihood compute reproduction number. stability MLEs assessed checking estimate \(R_0\) within 0.02 1. estimates \(\lambda\) \(\psi\) satisfy constraints specified psi_max, r0_min, r0_max, infectious_period_min, infectious_period_max parameters, MLEs \(\lambda\) \(\psi\) returned, unless force_two_step=TRUE case original estimates \(\lambda\) \(\psi\) returned regardless whether constraints satisfied. LTT regression fails MLEs \(\lambda\) \(\psi\) returned. MLEs obtained numerically using L-BFGS-S algorithm (see optim implementation details) n_starts times randomly chosen starting points selecting best maximizer attempts. Due user-imposed constraints, randomly generated starting points valid, 100*n_starts attempts made generate valid starting points. may result fewer n_starts optimizations actually performed. two-step method produces estimates violate constraints numerically unstable, function falls back MLE estimates step 1.

title: Estimate rate parameters of a birth-death-sampling process — estimate_bds_parameters
code: data(ebola_tree)  # Use the following information about the 2013 Ebola outbreak to obtain estimates # - Average removal rate of 5 # - Infectious periods range from 20 to 40 days # - An upper bound on the sampling rate of 15 # - Plausible values for the basic reproduction number are between 1.5 and 3 BDS_fit <- estimate_bds_parameters(ebola_tree, mu = 5,                                                psi_max = 15,                                                infectious_period_min = 20/365,                                                infectious_period_max = 40/365,                                                r0_min = 1.5,                                                r0_max = 3)

what: Estimate the transition rate matrix of the discrete trait process — estimate_transition_rates
title: Estimate the transition rate matrix of the discrete trait process — estimate_transition_rates
text: estimate_transition_rates() computes maximum likelihood estimate transition rate matrix \(Q\) governing discrete trait process. discrete traits evolve according Markov process transition rate matrix \(Q\) element \(q_{ij}\) instantaneous rate transitioning state \(\) state \(j\).

title: Estimate the transition rate matrix of the discrete trait process — estimate_transition_rates
code: estimate_transition_rates(tree, matrix_structure = "ER", method = "ace")

title: Estimate the transition rate matrix of the discrete trait process — estimate_transition_rates
text: tree phylo object tip.state attribute assigning traits tips. matrix_structure form transition rate matrix. Can either numeric matrix one following strings: "ER", "SYM", "ARD" (see details). default value "ER". method method used perform estimation. Possible values "ace" "fitMk".

title: Estimate the transition rate matrix of the discrete trait process — estimate_transition_rates
text: transition rate matrix fit observed phylogeny. matrix compatible saasi functions.

title: Estimate the transition rate matrix of the discrete trait process — estimate_transition_rates
text: model parameters controls structure \(Q\). "ER" specifies equal rates transitions, \(q_{ij}=q\) traits \(,j\). "SYM" specifies symmetric transition rate matrix, \(q_{ij}=q_{ji}\) traits \(,j\). "ARD" places structural constraints allows traits different, \(q_{ij}\ne q_{kl}\) traits \(,j,k,l\). another grouping transition rates desired can input numeric matrix entry matrix positive integer entries value share rate. method parameter allows user select preferred method ace fitMk, however preferred method fails used instead. ace selected, maximum likelihood estimator computed using ape::ace() function. fitMk selected, phytools::fitMk() function used fit \(Q\) provided phylogenetic tree.

title: Estimate the transition rate matrix of the discrete trait process — estimate_transition_rates
code: # Load a timed phylogenetic tree for Ebola data(ebola_tree)  # Use the simmap function to estimate the rate transition matrix # - Impose a symmetric form on the matrix Q <- estimate_transition_rates(ebola_tree, matrix_structure = "SYM")  # Use ace to estimate the rate transition matrix # - Impose a structure such that transitions to or from Guinea happen at a different rate than Liberia or Sierra Leone struct <- matrix(c(0, 1, 1, 1, 0, 2, 1, 2, 0), nrow=3, ncol=3) Q <- estimate_transition_rates(ebola_tree, matrix_structure = struct)

what: Prepare phylogenetic tree for saasi — prepare_tree_for_saasi
title: Prepare phylogenetic tree for saasi — prepare_tree_for_saasi
text: Prepare phylogenetic tree saasi

title: Prepare phylogenetic tree for saasi — prepare_tree_for_saasi
code: prepare_tree_for_saasi(   tree,   tip_data = NULL,   resolve_polytomies = TRUE,   fix_branches = TRUE,   min_branch_length = 1e-05,   drop_states = character(0) )

title: Prepare phylogenetic tree for saasi — prepare_tree_for_saasi
text: tree phylo object tip_data named vector names = tip labels, values = states, df 2 columns: column 1 = tip labels, column 2 = states. Optional tree already tip states resolve_polytomies Resolve polytomies multi2di fix_branches Fix zero/negative branch lengths min_branch_length Replace value zero negative branch lengths (default: 1e-5) drop_states States removed tree. NA always included. Add additional values drop states. Example:c("Collected").

title: Prepare phylogenetic tree for saasi — prepare_tree_for_saasi
text: prepared phylo object compatible SAASI.

what: Sampling Aware Ancestral State Inference — saasi
title: Sampling Aware Ancestral State Inference — saasi
text: Reconstructs ancestral states internal nodes phylogenetic tree accounting heterogeneous sampling rates across states locations.

title: Sampling Aware Ancestral State Inference — saasi
code: saasi(phy, Q, pars, sensitivity_test = FALSE)

title: Sampling Aware Ancestral State Inference — saasi
text: phy phylo object containing phylogenetic tree. tree must rooted, binary, contain tip.state. Q numeric transition rate matrix \(\exp{(n \times n)}\) n number states. Row column names represent states. -diagonal elements transition rates; diagonal elements set rows sum zero. pars data frame parameters used ancestral state reconstruction algorithm. Must following column names: state, prior, lambda, mu, psi. prior values refer baseline probabilities states (used root tree). sensitivity_test boolean indicating sensitivity test conducted. default value FALSE.

title: Sampling Aware Ancestral State Inference — saasi
text: data frame state probabilities internal node phy.

title: Sampling Aware Ancestral State Inference — saasi

what: Simulate a birth/death/sampling tree with post-processing — sim_bds_tree
title: Simulate a birth/death/sampling tree with post-processing — sim_bds_tree
text: tree can passed saasi, include speciation, extinction, sampling, mutation events. tree post-processed remove tips present ensure minimum number tips.

title: Simulate a birth/death/sampling tree with post-processing — sim_bds_tree
code: sim_bds_tree(   params_df,   q_matrix,   x0,   max_taxa = 100,   max_t = 100,   include_extinct = FALSE,   min_tip = 1 )

title: Simulate a birth/death/sampling tree with post-processing — sim_bds_tree
text: params_df Parameters used simulation. contain state names, speciation, extinction, sampling rates, x0 Natural number used root state returned tree. Must state declared params_df. max_taxa Maximum number nodes allowed initial simulated tree. max_t Maximum depth allowed returned tree. include_extinct Boolean declaring whether extinct taxa included returned tree. min_tip Minimum number tips required post-processed tree. processed tree fewer tips, simulation repeated condition satisfied. Default 1. q_martix Transition rate matrix.

title: Simulate a birth/death/sampling tree with post-processing — sim_bds_tree
text: phylo phylogenetic tree (ape format) post-processing applied. tree includes tip.state attribute character state labels.
